title: Aion Flow — The Gleam Workflow Authoring SDK
intention: Define aion_flow, the Gleam package (published to Hex) that workflow authors import to write durable workflows. It is the human-facing surface of the entire engine: a workflow author writes a plain, statically-typed Gleam function — decisions, loops, data transformation — and reaches the outside world only through this SDK's activity, signal, query, timer, and child-workflow primitives. The package is pure Gleam with no engine code inside it; every primitive is a thin @external(erlang) binding that resolves at runtime, when beamr loads the compiled workflow and the engine has registered the matching NIFs (AE-004), to the engine-side mechanics in AE/AD/AT. The defining property is compile-time type safety: activity inputs and outputs, signal payloads, and query returns are all statically typed, so an invalid composition — passing a String where an activity expects an Order, awaiting a signal of the wrong type, returning the wrong type from a query handler — fails at `gleam build`, not three days into a long-running production workflow. This is the concrete advantage over Temporal's SDKs (Go weakly typed, TypeScript better but runtime-checked). The package also ships aion/testing, the Temporal-equivalent test harness: simulate time so a month-long sleep resolves instantly, mock activities to return canned results, and assert that a workflow replays to the same state — all in plain gleam test, with no engine, no beamr, and no store.
problem: Workflow authors need a single, ergonomic, fully type-safe Gleam surface for the engine's primitives. Without it, an author would write raw @external bindings to engine NIFs by hand — losing types, losing the activity/signal/query/timer/child abstractions, and re-deriving the serialisation discipline every time.

Determinism is invisible but load-bearing: `workflow.now` and `workflow.random` must NOT read the wall clock or an entropy source — they must bind to AD's determinism machinery (recorded timestamp, seeded RNG). The SDK must make the deterministic path the only path an author can reach, so a correct-looking workflow cannot accidentally be non-deterministic by calling a Gleam stdlib clock.

The recorded/side-effectful boundary (DESIGN-OVERVIEW's core concept) must be expressed in the type surface: side effects are activities (recorded, replayed from history), deterministic computation is plain Gleam (re-run on replay). The SDK must make `activity.new` the obvious and only home for a side effect, and make running an activity (`workflow.run`) the recorded call.

Gleam values must cross the @external boundary to type-erased engine NIFs (which speak aion-core Payload bytes) and come back typed. The SDK must define how a typed Gleam value is encoded to the engine's payload form on the way out and decoded on the way back in, with a typed decode-failure path — without leaking the encoding into author code.

Errors from activities must carry the retryable-vs-terminal classification the engine consults (aion-core ActivityError), expressed in Gleam's type system rather than as a stringly-typed convention, so an author classifies a failure once and the engine's retry policy acts on it correctly.

Authors need to test workflows without a running engine. A month-long sleep, a signal that arrives on day seven, an activity that calls an LLM — none of these can be exercised in a normal unit test. The SDK must ship a test harness that simulates time, mocks activities, and asserts replay determinism, matching what Temporal's testing framework provides.

solution.summary: A pure Gleam package, aion_flow, under gleam/aion_flow, organised into author-facing modules (aion/workflow, aion/activity, aion/signal, aion/query, aion/child, aion/error, aion/testing) over a single internal binding layer (aion/internal/ffi) that holds every @external(erlang) declaration and the Payload codec. Author modules are typed, documented, and ergonomic; the ffi module is the one place that names engine NIFs and speaks the type-erased Payload form. Activities are values: `activity.new(name, input, run)` builds an Activity(input, output) carrying its name, its typed input, a Codec for output, and (for in-VM activities) the runner; `activity.retry`/`timeout`/`heartbeat` decorate it; `workflow.run(activity)` is the single recorded call that dispatches it via the NIF and decodes the typed result or a typed ActivityError. Determinism binds directly: `workflow.now()` and `workflow.random()` are @external to AD's determinism context, never Gleam clocks. Timers (`sleep`, `start_timer`, `cancel_timer`, `with_timeout`), signals (`receive`/`SignalRef` with a typed payload Codec), queries (`query.handler` registration returning a typed value), and children (`workflow.spawn`/`spawn_and_wait`, `child.await`, ChildHandle) each bind to their AT mechanism through ffi with the type carried in Gleam and the payload encoded/decoded at the boundary. Concurrency (`all`/`race`/`map`) are typed combinators over Activity/child values that bind to AT's selective-receive collectors. Errors use a RetryableError/TerminalError split that maps onto aion-core's ActivityError classification. aion/testing provides an in-Gleam harness: a simulated clock that advances logical time so sleeps resolve instantly, an activity mock registry that returns canned typed results without dispatching a NIF, and replay assertions that run a workflow twice over the same recorded history and assert identical observations — all without beamr, the engine, or a store.
solution.detail: Type safety is the through-line. An Activity(input, output) is parameterised by both its input and output types; workflow.run(Activity(i, o)) returns Result(o, ActivityError); a SignalRef(payload) makes workflow.receive return Result(payload, ReceiveError); a query handler registered for type a must return a; spawn(workflow_fn, input) where the workflow function is fn(i) -> Result(o, e) yields a ChildHandle(o, e). Mismatched compositions do not type-check. The Payload codec (Codec(a) = encode + decode over the ffi string/bytes form) is threaded through every primitive so the author supplies it once at the type boundary and never touches encoding again; a decode failure at the boundary is a typed error, never a panic. The @external binding mechanism is exactly the established pattern (see .meridian/workflows/onatopp-dev-gleam meridian_ffi.gleam): `@external(erlang, "aion_flow_ffi", "<name>")` declarations whose Erlang module is the NIF registry name the engine registers (AE-004); at compile time gleam type-checks against the Gleam signature, at runtime beamr resolves the call to the engine NIF. aion_flow declares the bindings and the types; it does not implement the NIFs (that is AE/AD/AT) and does not author NIFs in Rust (that is AN).

decisions.title: Pure Gleam package; one internal ffi module owns every @external
decisions.choice: aion_flow is a pure Gleam Hex package containing no Rust and no engine code. Every @external(erlang, "aion_flow_ffi", ...) declaration lives in a single internal module, aion/internal/ffi, which also holds the Payload codec helpers. Author-facing modules (workflow, activity, signal, query, child, error) call into ffi and never declare @external themselves. Rejected: scattering @external across the author modules — it would expose the NIF names and the type-erased payload form to every module and make the engine binding contract impossible to audit in one place.

decisions.title: @external bindings resolve to engine NIFs at runtime, not compile time
decisions.choice: Each ffi binding is `@external(erlang, "aion_flow_ffi", "<fn>")`. The Erlang module name "aion_flow_ffi" is the NIF registry namespace the engine registers via EngineBuilder::register_nifs (AE-004). gleam build type-checks the Gleam signatures with no engine present; beamr resolves the call to the registered NIF at runtime when the workflow executes. This is the established pattern from .meridian/workflows/onatopp-dev-gleam. Consequence: aion_flow has zero Rust dependency and publishes to Hex standalone; the binding is satisfied only inside a running engine. Rejected: a compile-time Rust FFI dependency — it would couple the Hex package to the engine crate and break the clean authoring/engine separation.

decisions.title: Activities are typed values, not strings
decisions.choice: activity.new(name: String, input: i, run: fn(i) -> Result(o, ActivityError)) builds an opaque Activity(i, o) carrying the name, the typed input, an output Codec, retry/timeout/heartbeat config, and the runner. The type parameters i and o make `workflow.run(activity)` return Result(o, ActivityError) with o known at compile time. Decorators (retry/timeout/heartbeat) return a new Activity(i, o), so they compose in a pipeline. Rejected: a stringly-typed activity-name + untyped Dynamic result (Temporal's weaker SDKs) — it pushes type errors to runtime, defeating the cluster's reason to exist.

decisions.title: workflow.run is the single recorded dispatch; plain Gleam is re-run on replay
decisions.choice: The only way to invoke an activity is workflow.run(activity) (and the concurrency combinators all/race/map, which run activities). workflow.run binds to the engine's activity dispatch (AE-008) + recording (AD), so its result is recorded and returned from history on replay. Plain Gleam computation in the workflow body is never recorded and is re-executed on replay (safe because deterministic). The SDK offers no other side-effect escape hatch, making the recorded/side-effectful boundary structural. Rejected: a `workflow.side_effect(fn)` convenience that records an arbitrary closure result — it invites authors to smuggle non-deterministic reads outside the activity contract.

decisions.title: now and random bind to AD's determinism context, never Gleam clocks
decisions.choice: workflow.now() -> Timestamp and workflow.random() -> Float (and random_int) are @external bindings to AD's determinism context (recorded event timestamp; seeded RNG keyed on WorkflowId+RunId per AD CO8/CO9). The SDK documents loudly that gleam stdlib's clock and any erlang:now/rand must never be called from workflow code, and provides the deterministic primitives so the author never needs them. Rejected: wrapping gleam_erlang's clock — it would read wall time and desynchronise replay.

decisions.title: A Codec(a) carries typed values across the type-erased boundary
decisions.choice: Every primitive that crosses the @external boundary with user data (activity input/output, signal payload, query return, child input/output, workflow input/output) takes a Codec(a) = #(encode: fn(a) -> String, decode: fn(String) -> Result(a, DecodeError)). The author supplies the codec once at the type boundary (typically a JSON encode/decode pair); the SDK encodes on the way out to the ffi string form and decodes on the way back, surfacing a typed DecodeError on failure — never a panic. The ffi string form maps to aion-core's Payload (JSON content-type) on the engine side. Rejected: requiring authors to hand-encode to strings at every call — it leaks the boundary into business logic; rejected: a global reflection-based serialiser — Gleam has no runtime reflection and it would erase the static guarantee.

decisions.title: Retryable vs terminal modelled as a Gleam type mapping aion-core ActivityError
decisions.choice: aion/error defines ActivityError with two constructors: Retryable(message, details) and Terminal(message, details). An activity runner returns Result(o, ActivityError); the engine consults the classification to apply the RetryPolicy (retry on Retryable until attempts exhausted, fail immediately on Terminal). This is the Gleam expression of aion-core's retryable/terminal split (aion-core C21). The SDK also surfaces engine-originated failures (timeout, cancellation, non-determinism) as typed variants distinct from author-returned ActivityError. Rejected: a bool retryable flag or a stringly error — it makes the contract implicit and easy to get backwards.

decisions.title: RetryPolicy and Backoff are typed data on the activity
decisions.choice: activity.retry(activity, RetryPolicy(max_attempts, backoff, ...)) and activity.timeout(activity, Duration) attach typed config to the Activity value. Backoff is a sum type: Exponential(initial, multiplier, max), Linear(initial, increment, max), Fixed(delay). The engine reads this config when dispatching; the SDK only carries it. No default policy is baked into the SDK — an activity with no retry decorator runs once (per the no-assumed-defaults rule); the author opts into retries explicitly. Rejected: a hidden default RetryPolicy — silent retries of a non-idempotent activity are a footgun, and defaults are a deployment-target decision (CLAUDE.md no-assumed-defaults).

decisions.title: Durations are a typed unit, constructed by named helpers
decisions.choice: A Duration type with constructors seconds/minutes/hours/days (and milliseconds) is the single time-quantity type used by sleep, with_timeout, activity.timeout, start_timer, and the backoff config. It carries a normalised internal representation (milliseconds) so the engine receives one canonical form. Named constructors (workflow.sleep(hours: 24) reads as `sleep(hours(24))` in Gleam labelled-argument style) keep call sites readable. Rejected: bare Int milliseconds at every call site — error-prone and unit-ambiguous.

decisions.title: Signals and queries carry typed payloads via SignalRef and typed handlers
decisions.choice: signal.new(name, Codec(payload)) -> SignalRef(payload); workflow.receive(signal_ref) -> Result(payload, ReceiveError) decodes the recorded SignalReceived payload to the typed value. query.handler(name, Codec(a), fn() -> a) registers a read-only handler whose return type a is fixed at registration; the engine's query service (AT) calls it and the SDK encodes the reply. A query handler is pure-read by type: it returns a value, it cannot run an activity (no workflow.run in handler scope by convention + documentation). Rejected: Dynamic signal payloads and stringly query names without a typed return — runtime type errors at the live-interaction surface, exactly where they are most expensive.

decisions.title: Children and concurrency are typed combinators over workflow/activity values
decisions.choice: workflow.spawn(name, workflow_fn: fn(i) -> Result(o, e), input: i, codecs) -> ChildHandle(o, e); workflow.spawn_and_wait(...) -> Result(o, e); child.await(handle) -> Result(o, e). The concurrency primitives are typed over a homogeneous list: workflow.all(List(Activity(i, o))) -> Result(List(o), ActivityError) (ordered), workflow.race(List(Activity(i, o))) -> Result(o, ActivityError) (first to settle — the first child to finish wins whether it succeeds or fails, matching the engine's AT-011 semantics), workflow.map(List(a), fn(a) -> Activity(i, o)) -> Result(List(o), ActivityError) (dynamic fan-out). They bind to AT's selective-receive collectors; the SDK carries the types and the per-element codecs. Rejected: heterogeneous tuple combinators in v1 — they complicate the type signatures; homogeneous lists cover the design's stated use (parallel same-type fan-out) and a typed-tuple `all2`/`all3` can come later.

decisions.title: aion/testing simulates time, mocks activities, and asserts replay — in pure Gleam
decisions.choice: The test harness runs entirely in gleam test with no beamr, engine, or store. A TestEnv holds a simulated clock (advance(duration) resolves pending sleeps/timers and returns control without wall-clock waiting), an activity mock registry (mock(name, fn(input) -> Result(output, ActivityError)) intercepts workflow.run for that activity and returns the canned typed result instead of dispatching a NIF), and a recorded-history capture used by replay assertions (run a workflow under the env, capture the observation sequence, replay it, and assert the two observation sequences are identical). The harness binds the same ffi surface to in-Gleam test implementations rather than engine NIFs via a concrete test double: a hand-written test/aion_flow_ffi.erl that implements the production 'aion_flow_ffi' NIF namespace in-process against a process-scoped TestEnv (process dictionary / ETS keyed by pid). gleam test loads it so the identical @external declarations resolve to it; the package ships no aion_flow_ffi in src/ (production resolves those names to engine-registered NIFs, D2). Author workflow code and every @external declaration are byte-identical between test and production — only which module backs the name at load time differs. Rejected: requiring a live engine for workflow tests — month-long sleeps and external signals cannot be exercised that way; this is exactly the gap Temporal's test framework fills.

decisions.title: Workflow entry contract is fn(input) -> Result(output, error) with declared codecs
decisions.choice: A workflow is a plain Gleam function fn(i) -> Result(o, e). It is made engine-runnable by workflow.define(name, input_codec, output_codec, error_codec, entry_fn) which returns an opaque WorkflowDefinition(i, o, e) carrying the name, the three codecs, and the entry function. The consumer is the engine (AE), not the Rust aion-package crate: at spawn AE calls the package's entry function (named in the manifest), receives the WorkflowDefinition, and uses the codecs to decode the input Payload and encode the result/error Payload across the type-erased boundary. aion-package records only the entry module and function NAMES (strings) in manifest.json — it never introspects the Gleam value. The SDK does not run the function itself. Rejected: an implicit `pub fn run(String) -> String` convention (the bootstrap pattern in onatopp-dev-gleam) as the only contract — it loses the static input/output types this cluster exists to provide; the typed define wraps it. Rejected (earlier wording): that the manifest 'consumes' the define value — the manifest carries names; the engine consumes the value.

constraints.description: Pure Gleam package. No Rust, no engine code, no beamr dependency inside aion_flow. It publishes to Hex standalone (gleam_stdlib is its only runtime dependency, plus gleam_json for the default JSON codecs).
Every @external(erlang, ...) declaration lives in the single internal module aion/internal/ffi. No author-facing module declares an @external (per D1).

Module files contain logic; the package root and any mod-equivalent re-export aggregator stay declarations/re-exports only. No god files — split before a module exceeds ~500 lines of code (excluding comments/whitespace).

No partial implementations. Every public function is complete and total: no panic, no todo, no assert-as-control-flow; failure paths return typed Result errors. Gleam has no exceptions for control flow — every fallible binding returns Result.

Type safety is enforced, not advisory: activity input/output, signal payload, query return, and child input/output types are static type parameters; an invalid composition fails `gleam build`. A type-safety test brief asserts representative invalid compositions are rejected (compile-fail expectations documented).

Determinism: the SDK exposes workflow.now/workflow.random bound to AD's determinism context and exposes NO binding to a wall clock or entropy source. Workflow-code documentation states the prohibition explicitly.

The recorded/side-effectful boundary is structural: the only side-effect dispatch the SDK offers is workflow.run(activity) (and all/race/map over activities). There is no generic side_effect(fn) escape hatch (per D4).

No hardcoded defaults for configurable values: no default RetryPolicy, no default timeout, no default backoff numbers baked into the SDK. An activity without a retry decorator runs once; the author supplies all policy values (CLAUDE.md no-assumed-defaults).
User data crosses the boundary only through a Codec(a); a decode failure is a typed DecodeError, never a panic. No primitive silently swallows a decode error.

aion_flow does not implement engine mechanics (timer firing, signal routing, replay) — those are AE/AD/AT, reached via @external. It does not author NIFs in Rust — that is AN. It is not packaged into .aion — that is AP. The cluster owns only the Gleam API surface and its binding declarations.
All acceptance uses Gleam tooling: gleam build, gleam check, gleam format --check, gleam test must all pass clean. The test harness (aion/testing) runs under gleam test with no external services.

goals: A pure Gleam Hex package aion_flow whose single internal ffi module declares every @external(erlang, "aion_flow_ffi", ...) binding and the Payload codec, with no @external in any author-facing module.

aion/activity: activity.new building a typed Activity(input, output); retry/timeout/heartbeat decorators; RetryPolicy and Backoff (Exponential | Linear | Fixed) as typed data carried on the activity with no baked-in defaults.

aion/workflow: run (the single recorded dispatch returning the typed result or a typed ActivityError); now and random bound to AD's determinism context; sleep/start_timer/cancel_timer/with_timeout over a typed Duration; spawn/spawn_and_wait; all/race/map typed concurrency combinators.
aion/signal: signal.new -> SignalRef(payload); typed receive decoding the recorded SignalReceived payload; the send helper for the in-engine/client side as a typed binding.
aion/query: typed query handler registration (handler returns a fixed type a) and reply, binding to AT's query service; no event recorded for a query.
aion/child: spawn, await, ChildHandle(output, error) as typed handles over child-workflow executions.
aion/error: ActivityError with Retryable/Terminal constructors mapping aion-core's classification, plus typed engine-originated failures (timeout, cancellation, non-determinism, decode).
A Duration type with seconds/minutes/hours/days/milliseconds constructors normalised to a canonical form, used by every time-quantity argument.
A Codec(a) abstraction threaded through every typed boundary, with default JSON codec helpers and a typed DecodeError.

aion/testing: a TestEnv with a simulated clock (advance resolves sleeps/timers instantly), an activity mock registry (canned typed results without NIF dispatch), and replay assertions (run-twice-over-history, assert identical observations) — all in pure gleam test.

A workflow.define returning an opaque WorkflowDefinition(input, output, error) carrying the entry function plus input/output/error codecs (the typed contract the engine consumes at spawn; the .aion manifest records only its module/function names), and an end-to-end typed example workflow demonstrating the full surface compiling and passing gleam test under the harness.
non_goals: No engine mechanics — timer firing, signal routing into mailboxes, query dispatch, child spawning, replay, and determinism context are AE/AD/AT. This cluster declares the @external bindings to them and the Gleam types; it does not implement them.
No Rust NIF authoring — writing/registering the NIFs that aion_flow_ffi names is aion-nif (AN) and the engine (AE-004). This cluster names the bindings; it does not implement the native side.

No .aion packaging — compiling the Gleam to .beam and bundling into a .aion archive is aion-package (AP). This cluster's workflow.define provides the typed entry contract the engine invokes at spawn; the manifest records its module/function names; this cluster does not build the archive.

No network transport, client, or server — starting/signalling/querying a workflow from outside is the client SDKs (AL) over aion-server (AW). aion/signal's send helper is the in-engine/Gleam-client typed binding, not an HTTP client.
No Elixir SDK — aion_flow_ex is a later phase with its own package.
No event-store, no persistence — the SDK never touches a store; it crosses to the engine via @external only.
No remote (Tier-3) worker SDK — out-of-process activities in other languages are AR.

structure.gleam/aion_flow/gleam.toml: [AF-001] package manifest: name, version, gleam_stdlib + gleam_json deps, Hex metadata
structure.gleam/aion_flow/src/aion_flow.gleam: [AF-001] top-level package doc + curated re-exports (the public surface)
structure.gleam/aion_flow/src/aion/internal/ffi.gl: [AF-001, AF-002] the single @external(erlang, "aion_flow_ffi", ...) binding layer for every engine NIF
structure.gleam/aion_flow/src/aion/codec.gleam: [AF-002] Codec(a) type, default JSON codec helpers, DecodeError
structure.gleam/aion_flow/src/aion/activity.gleam: [AF-004] Activity(i, o), activity.new, retry/timeout/heartbeat, RetryPolicy, Backoff
structure.gleam/aion_flow/src/aion/workflow.gleam: [AF-005, AF-006, AF-009, AF-010] workflow module declarations + re-exports (run, now, random, sleep, timers, with_timeout, spawn, all/race/map, define)
structure.gleam/aion_flow/src/aion/workflow/run.gl: [AF-005] workflow.run (recorded activity dispatch) + now + random (determinism bindings)
[AF-006] sleep, start_timer, cancel_timer, with_timeout over Duration
structure.gleam/aion_flow/src/aion/workflow/define: [AF-005] workflow.define: typed entry contract (entry fn + input/output/error codecs)
structure.gleam/aion_flow/src/aion/signal.gleam: [AF-007] signal.new, SignalRef(payload), typed receive, typed send helper
structure.gleam/aion_flow/src/aion/child.gleam: [AF-009] ChildHandle(o, e), spawn, await; workflow.spawn/spawn_and_wait wrappers
structure.gleam/aion_flow/src/aion/testing.gleam: [AF-011] testing module declarations + re-exports (TestEnv, mock, advance, replay assertions)
structure.gleam/aion_flow/src/aion/testing/clock.g: [AF-011] simulated clock: advance resolves pending sleeps/timers instantly
structure.gleam/aion_flow/src/aion/testing/mock.gl: [AF-011] activity mock registry: canned typed results intercepting workflow.run
[AF-011] replay assertions: run-twice-over-history, assert identical observations
structure.gleam/aion_flow/test/aion_flow_test.glea: [AF-012] type-safety + harness + end-to-end example workflow tests
structure.gleam/aion_flow/test/aion_flow_ffi.erl: [AF-011] test-only Erlang double for the aion_flow_ffi NIF namespace (in-process, process-scoped TestEnv)
[AF-012] compile-fail checker: runs gleam check on negative fixtures, asserts type-mismatch rejection
